analysis.period_summary: For the week of 2026-04-20 to 2026-04-26, the funnel generated $3,412.60 USD net revenue from 97 frontend sales, with a total backend stack (including upsells) contributing an additional $1,536.39 in gross upsell revenue before refunds. The single most critical finding is that the checkout-to-purchase rate is severely broken across all major flows, ranging from 1.63% to 13.33% against a 20-30% target, costing dozens of sales per week from already-engaged buyers who clicked the ClickBank CTA.

funnel_scorecard.commentary: The funnel opts in well (21-26% landing-to-optin, at or above the 15-25% healthy benchmark) and drives strong checkout click-through (82-91%), but the ClickBank checkout completion rate is critically low across every meaningful flow, with the best-performing flow (c→offer_v4) still only reaching 13.33%. OTO take rates are also well below benchmarks, suggesting both the checkout experience and post-purchase sequence need urgent attention.

funnel_leaks.why_it_matters: With 1,745 total checkout clicks this week and only 97 purchases, achieving even a 15% checkout completion rate would yield approximately 261 sales — a ~169% revenue lift from traffic already in hand.

The /offer/v4-v3 page loses more than half of all visitors before they even click the CTA, performing far below the 80%+ benchmark, meaning campaign 107 is wasting 66 potential checkout attempts per week.
At 18.6% OTO1 take rate versus a 40%+ strong benchmark, the funnel is leaving roughly $1,400 in OTO1 revenue on the table weekly assuming current frontend sales volume and $67 price point.
OTO2 at 8.2% is nearly half the 15% minimum benchmark, and the combined OTO2 + downsell take rate of 9.2% suggests the Timeline offer sequence or positioning is failing to convert post-OTO1 buyers.
Landing /b has a 6.67% optin rate against the 15-25% healthy benchmark, though sample size is only 15 views, so this should be confirmed with more traffic before taking corrective action.

upsell_analysis.commentary: Backend monetization is significantly underperforming: OTO1 combined (full + downsell) reaches only 21.6% against a 40%+ target, and OTO2 combined hits just 9.3% against a 25%+ target. High refund rates on OTO1 (5 of 18 full-price sales refunded = 27.8% refund rate) and OTO2 (4 of 8 = 50% refund rate) are compressing net backend revenue and suggest possible offer-to-buyer mismatch or delivery issues downstream of purchase.

needle_movers.why: 1,745 checkout clicks produced only 97 purchases (5.6% blended rate), meaning 1,648 buyers who voluntarily clicked the CTA did not complete. At a conservative 15% target rate, that gap represents approximately 165 additional sales worth ~$6,105 in revenue at $37 AOV from this week's traffic alone.
what: Audit the ClickBank order form page: check load speed, mobile rendering, form field count, and trust signals (SSL badge, guarantee copy)
A/B test a simplified or pre-filled order form and consider adding a progress indicator or urgency element on the checkout page
Review ClickBank cart abandonment data to identify where specifically users drop (payment page vs. form entry vs. confirmation)
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Improving checkout completion from ~6% to 15% on 1,745 weekly checkout clicks = ~157 additional sales × $37 = ~$5,809 additional frontend revenue per week

needle_movers.area: OTO1 take rate and refund rate
needle_movers.why: OTO1 Blueprint is converting at 18.6% (target 40%+) and has a 27.8% refund rate on full-price sales, indicating both a presentation problem on the OTO page and a possible expectation mismatch once buyers receive the product.
what: Rewrite OTO1 headline and lead to directly connect Blueprint value to the just-purchased main product, reinforcing momentum
Add specific outcome-based bullet points and a concrete use-case demonstration to the OTO1 VSL or TSL
Review Blueprint delivery and content quality against buyer expectations; if refunds are driven by disappointment, fix the product before scaling
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting OTO1 take rate from 18.6% to 30% on 97 sales = ~11 additional OTO1 sales × $67 = ~$737 incremental per week; reducing refunds from 27.8% to 10% on current sales recovers ~$228 net weekly

needle_movers.why: The /offer/v4-v3 page has a 47.62% offer-to-checkout rate versus 82-91% on all other offer variants, meaning it is losing 32-43 percentage points of potential checkout clicks per optin despite adequate optin volume (126 completions this week).
what: Compare /offer/v4-v3 CTA placement, button copy, and page length against /offer/v4/ which achieves 84-91% — identify structural differences
Test moving the primary CTA higher on /offer/v4-v3 or adding a secondary CTA mid-page to capture readers who do not reach the bottom
If v4-v3 is an intentional test variant, consider pausing campaign 107 and redirecting its traffic to the best-performing offer (v4-v2 or v4)
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Matching /offer/v4-v3's CTA rate to 84% (the flow average) would yield ~46 additional checkout clicks; at current 3.33% purchase rate that is ~1.5 extra sales, but CTA improvement combined with checkout fix could yield 6-7 additional sales from this traffic

needle_movers.area: OTO2 take rate and refund rate
needle_movers.why: OTO2 Timeline converts at 8.2% with a 50% gross refund rate (4 refunds on 8 sales), effectively making it a near-zero net revenue contributor, suggesting either the offer is misaligned with the buyer journey at that point or the product has significant delivery issues.
what: Audit OTO2 page for clarity of value proposition relative to OTO1 — ensure it feels additive, not redundant
Investigate why 50% of OTO2 buyers are refunding: send a survey to refunders or review support tickets to identify the core complaint
Test lowering the OTO2 full price or strengthening the OTO2 downsell offer to recover more buyers who decline at $47
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting OTO2 take rate to 15% (minimum benchmark) on 97 buyers = ~7 additional OTO2 sales × $47 = ~$329 additional per week; halving the refund rate recovers ~$84 net weekly

needle_movers.area: v4 vs v4-v2 offer split — checkout-to-purchase gap
needle_movers.why: Across the two highest-volume comparable flows (d→offer_v4 vs d→offer_v4_v2), /offer/v4-v2 achieves 7.57% checkout-to-purchase vs 4.06% for /offer/v4/ — nearly double — suggesting offer v4-v2 is better at converting buyers who reach ClickBank, though both remain critically below target.
what: Prioritise routing higher-volume campaigns to /offer/v4-v2 until checkout completion is fixed, as it generates nearly double the purchase rate at comparable CTA click volumes
Analyse what differs between v4 and v4-v2 at the checkout stage (price anchoring, guarantee copy, urgency) to extract winning elements
Monitor d-v3 flows (9.13% on v4-v2 vs 5.58% on v4) which confirm the same directional pattern, strengthening the case to favour v4-v2
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Shifting all current /offer/v4/ checkout clicks (~706 this week) to the v4-v2 conversion rate (~7.5%) from current v4 rate (~4.5%) = approximately 21 additional purchases × $37 = ~$777 incremental revenue per week

cpv_cb_discrepancy.note: CPV Labs is under-counting by 5 purchases (92 vs 97 ClickBank frontend sales), most likely due to missing postback fires on some transactions; the $680.99 USD revenue gap is partly attributable to the SGD-to-USD conversion approximation and ClickBank fee netting, but the missing postbacks should be investigated to ensure campaign optimisation decisions are not made on incomplete conversion data.

analysis.data_notes: 1) The 'landing→offer' flow shows 3 landing views but 320 offer page views — this is a clear tracking anomaly where the offer path '/offer' is receiving direct or untracked traffic not attributed to a landing page; the 0.62% offer-to-checkout rate on this flow may reflect bot or misconfigured traffic and should be excluded from optimisation decisions. 2) GA4 funnel_v4 shows landing_to_optin_page_pct of 1,122.56%, which is impossible and indicates a GA4 event firing or session attribution error — GA4 supplement data for funnel_v4 should not be used for landing-to-optin-page rate calculations this period, though the optin_page_to_submit_pct of 43.78% (form shown → submitted) is plausible if treated in isolation. 3) Flows a→?, b_c→?, f→offer_v4, and f→offer_v4_v2 all have negligible volume (1-8 views) and cannot be meaningfully analysed. 4) ClickBank OTO2 refund rate of 50% (4 of 8 sales) is flagged as anomalously high and warrants immediate investigation into delivery or fulfilment. 5) CPV revenue is denominated in SGD and has been converted at 0.74 SGD/USD for cross-check purposes only — do not compare CPV campaign revenue figures directly to ClickBank USD amounts.
